requirement: Behavioral Baselines: Establish and maintain profiles of normal activity for systems and models.
Response Alignment: Ensure alerts trigger appropriate incident playbooks.
Integration: Feed anomaly detections into centralized monitoring and incident systems.
Validation & Tuning: Periodically test detection accuracy and retrain models; review and tune at least annually.

Cross-Domain Anomaly Correlation: Implement logic to correlate anomalies across the network, application, and AI inference layers (e.g., matching a spike in outbound traffic with a cluster of unusual model prompts) to identify coordinated attacks.
Detection Methods: Use ML, rules, and heuristics to identify anomalies and ethical risks (e.g., bias shifts).
Telemetry Collection: Aggregate logs, metrics, and AI inference data across systems and environments.
Alerting & Escalation: Define thresholds and escalation criteria for deviations or anomalies.
Baseline & Drift Detection: Define normal operational baselines for systems and models; monitor for performance drift and fairness bias.
Automation & Correlation: Integrate monitoring data into centralized SIEM/SOAR or AI observability platforms.
Retention & Integrity: Protect and retain monitoring data per regulatory and evidentiary standards.
Governance Oversight: Report monitoring outcomes to governance committees and integrate into audit planning.

Downstream Impact Monitoring: Monitor for unexpected spikes in negative real-world outcomes (e.g., disproportionate denials, safety reports, or legal inquiries) to detect model failures that internal statistical metrics may fail to capture.
Universal Coverage: 24/7 monitoring coverage for all critical assets and models; thresholds reviewed quarterly.
IOC Feed Integration: Subscribe to authoritative feeds (ISACs, threat intel services).
Response Workflows: Define playbooks for confirmed IOCs.
Correlation & Analysis: Automate IOC correlation with system telemetry.
Validation: Validate IOC coverage and accuracy regularly.
Reporting: Escalate verified IOCs through the incident response process.

AI-Specific Threat Indicators: Curate and monitor for "AI-native" IOCs, including known adversarial prompt signatures, anomalous model-inversion traffic patterns, and unique signatures associated with "poisoned" dataset repositories.
IOC database updated weekly; automated IOC correlation for all monitored assets.
Incident Classification: Define types and severity levels (including AI-specific categories like "Model Hijacking" or "Data Poisoning").
Reporting & Escalation: Establish clear channels; fulfill regulatory notification obligations (GDPR, AI Act).
Root Cause Analysis (RCA): Identify underlying vulnerabilities and control gaps immediately following containment.
Response Coordination: Integrate SOC, legal, PR, and communications functions.

AI Forensic Capture: Implement procedures to freeze and export the specific model version, weights, and inference telemetry (input/output/metadata) involved in an incident to enable deterministic replay and analysis in a secure environment.
24/7 Response & PIR: 24/7 incident reporting channel; post-incident review (PIR) within 10 business days of closure.
Evidence Review: Require attestations, SOC/ISO/AI assurance reports.
Issue Escalation: Track and remediate vendor-related incidents; define communication protocols for breaches.
Continuous Monitoring: Use automated tools for vendor security scoring and public vulnerability monitoring.
Ethical Oversight: Validate AI suppliers for fairness, transparency, and accountability practices.

Sub-Processor & API Discovery: Implement automated traffic analysis or CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) tools to identify where third-party vendors are forwarding organizational data to secondary sub-processors or external AI APIs.
Full Compliance Coverage: All critical vendors have an active risk score and current assurance documentation.
Independence: Use qualified assessors independent from operations.
Assessment Planning: Define annual schedule covering all system types.
Fully Documented Testing Program: Conduct vulnerability, code(e.g., SAST, DAST, IAST, SCA...) tests.
Tracking & Remediation: Document findings and verify closure.
Integration: Feed results into risk management and audit functions.
Ongoing comprehensive assessment cycle; all findings tracked to closure within SLA. (e.g., Weekly Cadence with Reporting)
Define annual scope covering both standard infrastructure (Cloud, API) and AI components (Models, RAG pipelines, Training Data).
Standard Execution: Conduct standard penetration testing on hosting infrastructure, APIs, and web applications.
AI Execution: Conduct manual adversarial testing targeting AI logic (e.g., Jailbreaking, Prompt Injection, Extraction).
Remediation: Document findings from all testing streams and track to closure within SLA.
Automation AI Fuzzing: Implement automated adversarial evaluation tools (e.g., Garak, PyRIT) for continuous model testing.
Scenario Simulation: Simulate attacks on the AI supply chain, including model poisoning and third-party library compromise.
Thresholds & SLAs: Defined severity levels (e.g., CVSS scores) and mandatory remediation timelines (e.g., Critical < 7 days, High < 30 days).
Scope: Maintain an automated inventory of all assets (Cloud, On-prem) and AI artifacts (Model Registries, Training Servers) to ensure 100% coverage.
Execution: Conduct weekly automated vulnerability scans on all critical systems and identified AI infrastructure.
AI Specific Supply Chain Scanning Scan: AI development environments for vulnerable dependencies (e.g., malicious PyPI packages, outdated ML libraries).
emediation: Integration   Integrate scanning tools with ticketing systems (e.g., Jira) and patch management to assign findings to owners automatically
Pipeline CI/CD & Model Ops: Embed scanning into the software (CI/CD) and Model (MLOps) delivery pipelines to block vulnerable builds before deployment.
Analysis Risk Context: Prioritize remediation based on threat intelligence and asset criticality (e.g., "Is this AI server internet-facing?") rather than just raw CVSS score.
Strategy Annual Schedule: Define an annual calendar of exercises ensuring coverage of Cyber, IT Disaster Recovery, and AI System failures.
Roles: Identify and assign cross-functional participants (Legal, PR, IT, Data Science/AI Heads) for each scenario type.
Execution: Conduct discussion-based (tabletop) exercises to walk through playbooks and decision-making pathways.
AI Scenario: Simulate specific AI failures: Model Poisoning (Integrity), Inference Latency (Availability), or Mass Hallucination (Reputation).
Document "Lessons Learned" and assign corrective actions (CAPA) with deadlines.
Update the actual Response Playbooks and BCP documents based on the gaps found during the exercise.
Drift ValidationMove beyond tabletops to technical simulations (e.g., actual failover of systems, restoring from vaulted backups).
Defined the "No-Go" policy: Critical severity findings must fail the CI/CD build automatically (Blocking Gate).
Scope Repository Inventory: Maintain an automated inventory of all source code repositories, including "Sandbox" AI environments and Data Science repos.
Execution SAST/SCA: Integrate Static Analysis (SAST) and Dependency Scanning (SCA) into the standard application CI/CD pipeline.
Execution: Implement specialized scanners   to detect code flaws and hardcoded secrets often missed by standard SAST.
Integrate findings directly into developer workflows (e.g., Pull Request comments) to verify remediation before code merge.
Implement pre-commit hooks or pipeline scanners specifically to detect and block API Keys (OpenAI, AWS) and Credentials.
Conduct Dynamic Analysis (DAST) on running applications/APIs to validate that code vulnerabilities are actually exploitable.
AI Artifacts Model Scanning: Scan model binary files (e.g., .pkl, .h5) for malware or deserialization attacks before ingestion.
Sources & Validation: Subscribe to validated TI feeds (CISA, ISACs, vendors).
Integration: Feed TI into patching, monitoring, and training workflows.
Dissemination: Distribute actionable TI to relevant teams.
Feedback Loop: Update risk register with new threat categories.

Adversarial AI TTP Mapping: Map incoming threat intelligence to the MITRE ATLAS framework to identify specific tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) targeting the organization's AI model lifecycle and inference endpoints.
Analysis & Correlation: Map external intelligence to internal vulnerabilities.
Weekly review of intelligence bulletins; monthly updates to threat landscape assessments.
